question: On the displayed image, which quadrant contains the finding?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image for visible landmarks and the specific finding. I see the label "AS" (alveolar space) on the right side, a dotted white line that outlines an alveolar septal border, and a white arrowhead pointing to a bright green cell located along that dotted border. The green label "Muc1" is visible on the left margin, confirming the fluorescent signal is shown on the left side of the image. There is also a separate inset (a zoomed-in view) positioned in the lower right corner of the figure.

Next, I relate these observations to the question, which asks which quadrant contains the finding. I mentally divide the main image into four quadrants. The arrowhead-marked cell and the dotted outline of the septum are situated above the horizontal midline and to the left of the vertical midline of the main image.

Then I consider how that location corresponds to the answer choices. The finding is therefore in the upper-left area of the main image (the inset in the lower right is only a magnified detail, not the original location).

Based on the position of the arrowhead-marked cell (above center and left of center), the finding is in the upper left quadrant.
caption:
CD209L is expressed in lung as well as renal epithelial
and endothelial
cells: PFA fixed human lung, renal, and temporal arteriole tissues
were subjected to immunofluorescence staining. Lung tissue stained
with anti-MUC1, anti-CD31, and anti-CD209L antibodies. (A) Type II
alveoli epithelial cells of alveoli were positive for CD209L (red)
and MUC1 (green). (B) Pulmonary arteriole endothelial cells were positive
for CD31 (green) and CD209L (red). (C) Endothelial cells of the temporal
arteriole were stained with CD209L (green) and CD31 (red). (D) Renal
endothelial cells were positive for CD209L (red) and CD31 (green).
White arrowhead points to the alveolar cell, AS = alveolar space,
and white dotted line corresponds to alveolar septa. Image magnification,
white bars = 50 μm.
sub_caption: (A) Type II alveoli epithelial cells of alveoli were positive for CD209L (red) and MUC1 (green). White arrowhead points to the alveolar cell, AS = alveolar space, and white dotted line corresponds to alveolar septa.
image_context:
To investigate the potential involvement of CD209L and CD209 in
COVID-19, we first examined expression of CD209L in human tissues
from SARS-CoV-2 target organs, which include lung endothelial and
epithelial cells, renal vessels, tubules, glomeruli, and the temporal
artery, via immunofluorescence staining. Lung tissue was costained
with antibodies against CD209L and MUC1, the latter serving as a marker
for type II alveolar cells,41,42 and was examined using
laser microscopy. Isotope-labeled antibody served as a control and
showed negligible staining of the lung tissue (S. Figure 1A). The specificity of CD209L antibody was validated
in cell culture in HUVEC-TERT cells. Knockdown of CD209L eliminated
the immunoreactivity of CD209L antibody in Western blotting (S. Figure 1B). Our results showed prominent expression
of CD209L in the MUC1 positive alveolar cells (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">Figure <xref rid="fig1" ref-type="fig">1</xref></xref>A). We also observed expression of CD209L
in pulmonary capillaries (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">1</xref>A). We also observed expression of CD209L
in pulmonary capillaries (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">Figure <xref rid="fig1" ref-type="fig">1</xref></xref>B), the endothelium of the small and medium sized temporal
artery (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">1</xref>B), the endothelium of the small and medium sized temporal
artery (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">Figure <xref rid="fig1" ref-type="fig">1</xref></xref>C),
and renal arterioles (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">1</xref>C),
and renal arterioles (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">Figure <xref rid="fig1" ref-type="fig">1</xref></xref>D). CD31 served as a marker of endothelial cells (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">1</xref>D). CD31 served as a marker of endothelial cells (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">Figure <xref rid="fig1" ref-type="fig">1</xref></xref>B–D. Moreover,
we show that CD209L is expressed in renal proximal tubular epithelial
cells, which were marked by aquaporin 1 (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">1</xref>B–D. Moreover,
we show that CD209L is expressed in renal proximal tubular epithelial
cells, which were marked by aquaporin 1 (S. Figure 2A). However, CD209L was not observed in the glomerular capillaries,
and minimal expression was noted in the mesangial area, probably in
the infiltrating immune cells (S. Figure 2B). The results demonstrate the prominent expression of CD209L in
type II alveolar cells and pulmonary endothelium as well as renal
vessels and renal tubular cells, which are also potential target cells
of SARS-CoV-2. CD209 was expressed only in a subset of type MUC1 positive II alveolar
cells (S. Figure 2A). Furthermore, unlike
CD209L, which is highly expressed in endothelial cells (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">Figure <xref rid="fig1" ref-type="fig">1</xref></xref>C,D), we did not observe expression
of CD209 in the pulmonary or renal arterioles (data not shown). A
distinct expression of CD209 was observed in the limited proximal
tubular epithelial cells in kidneys (<xref rid="oc0c01537_0001" ref-type="fig">1</xref>C,D), we did not observe expression
of CD209 in the pulmonary or renal arterioles (data not shown). A
distinct expression of CD209 was observed in the limited proximal
tubular epithelial cells in kidneys (S. Figure 3B), but glomerular capillaries were also mostly negative for
CD209 (S. Figure 3C). Overall, these results
suggest that CD209 has a limited expression profile compared to CD209L
in the organs examined.
relevant_image_context: Lung tissue was costained with antibodies against CD209L and MUC1, the latter serving as a marker for type II alveolar cells,41,42 and was examined using laser microscopy. Isotope-labeled antibody served as a control and showed negligible staining of the lung tissue (S. Figure 1A). Our results showed prominent expression of CD209L in the MUC1 positive alveolar cells (Figure 1A). We also observed expression of CD209L in pulmonary capillaries (Figure 1A).
